title: 'Surviving on reserves': Housing organisation's 50-year legacy at risk
author: Peter Lusted
fullText: A not-for-profit housing organisation that has helped Canberra's homeless people and low-income earners for more than 50 years is at risk, after having its funding halved.ACT Shelter began in 1975, set up by a team of around 30 people, including Cathi Moore."We represented the voice of the poorest, the homeless, and the most disadvantaged in the housing sectors at that time," Ms Moore said.The organisation has a history of combining research and work with charities like the St Vincent de Paul Society and the Salvation Army, to advocate to government for policy change.Cathi Moore, who helped set up ACT Shelter, says its five-decade legacy shows its commitment to housing for low-income Canberrans. (ABC News: Stuart Carnegie)"We were working towards getting a tenant's voice in the development of policy, the bureaucracy was often quite removed from the people they were providing housing for," Ms Moore said."There were some very well-known campaigns about saving Havelock House, the long stay caravan park [and] trying to ensure that the needs of the most disadvantaged [were met], particularly in areas like disability."We have a fantastic legacy and have been here for 50 years, which shows the commitment of people to housing action for low-income people."But it's feared that legacy could be in danger, due to recent funding cuts.Five-decade legacy in doubtACT Shelter CEO Corinne Dobson said their funding was nearly halved in 2024."Since then, we've really cut back to the barest minimum," she said."We've been surviving on reserves."A grant under the territory government in 2021-22 saw the organisation receive an average of $380,000 per year over the three financial years.The funding allowed ACT Shelter to employ a full-time CEO, a small amount of staff, and provided office space."It was part of the governing agreement between the Labor and Greens, and the understanding was that funding would provide ongoing peak body funding to ACT Shelter," Ms Dobson said."However it was time limited, and it stopped in 2024."Corinne Dobson says without more funding ACT Shelter will have to lose staff and its office, and will not be able to employ a full-time CEO. (ABC News: Stuart Carnegie)The end of the grant means the organisation's funding dropped to $192,000 per year.In its 2026-27 budget submission to the ACT government, ACT Shelter requested an additional $198,000 funding per year to restore it to a "viable and sustainable footing"."Without that funding we will have to lose staff, our office, and we won't be able to continue to employ a full-time CEO," Ms Dobson said.An spokesperson said the ACT government is "considering future investment and spending decisions in the context of the 2026-27 budget". "Future investments beyond the current financial year will be assessed as part of that process," they said.Impact of losing ACT ShelterMs Dobson said having ACT Shelter's role reduced would have a negative impact on Canberrans who are homeless or on low-incomes."Unlike other states and territories, the ACT doesn't have a peak body for homelessness, we don't have a peak body for tenants, and we don't have a community housing peak either," she said."So a lot of those roles that are played by other organisations in other states, they all fall on ACT Shelter here."She said it's important to provide an independent community voice "so we're not seeing policy just simply being driven by private interests or property developers".St Vincent de Paul Society Canberra/Goulburn CEO, Lucy Hohnen, said ACT Shelter plays a vital role in the sector, and is relatively underfunded compared to shelters in other states and territories."The issues around homelessness and lack of housing in the ACT at the moment are dire, and we're seeing an increase on the numbers of people coming to us here at Vinnies for assistance with housing and homelessness requirements," Ms Hohnen said."ACT Shelter plays a really important role, they provide the representation of what we're seeing at the frontline."Lucy Hohnen says ACT Shelter plays an important role in gathering insight into the problems homeless and low-income Canberrans are facing. (ABC News: David Sciasci)Ms Hohnen reiterated the range of voices ACT Shelter represents, including clients, tenants and organisations like Vinnies."Without them being able to put their independent lens across all that we're seeing, the ACT government is not really going to get the intel and the insights that they need to design policies to prevent homelessness," she said.Cathi Moore said she was saddened by the struggle ACT Shelter has been faced with, and stressed its role is as important as it's ever been."The disadvantaged in Canberra — because of our higher median income community — are very disadvantaged, and so they're things we still have to keep working on," she said."[That] is why we need a well-funded peak body in Canberra."
summary: A not-for-profit housing organisation that has been helping low-income and homeless Canberrans for over 50 years says its future is at risk after having its funding almost halved.
